name: ipyleaflet (Interactive Leaflet Maps in Jupyter)

auth.notes: Most tile providers free; some require API keys (Mapbox, Google)

performance.notes: JavaScript Leaflet in browser; Python-JS sync via ipywidgets

agent_readiness.notes: Leaflet.js maps as Jupyter widgets. from ipyleaflet import Map, Marker, Polygon, CircleMarker, GeoData, TileLayer, DrawControl, MeasureControl, WidgetControl. m = Map(center=[37.7749, -122.4194], zoom=10). marker = Marker(location=[37.7749, -122.4194], draggable=True). m.add_layer(marker). display(m). GeoData: m.add_layer(GeoData(geo_dataframe=gdf, style={'color': 'blue'})). DrawControl for drawing on map. WMS layer: m.add_layer(WMSLayer(url=..., layers=...)). Heatmap: from ipyleaflet import Heatmap. Choropleth. Two-way Python-JS sync: marker.location updates Python when user drags. Layout: m.layout.width = '100%'. HBox/VBox with other widgets.

gotchas: Jupyter-only — requires Jupyter Notebook or Lab (not plain Python)
Two-way sync with JavaScript can be fragile — state may desync
Large GeoDataFrames slow down browser — simplify geometries first
No type annotations — limited IDE support
Export to static HTML is less clean than folium
best_when: Interactive Leaflet maps in Jupyter with Python-JavaScript two-way sync
Drawing tools for spatial data collection or editing
Combining maps with other ipywidgets (sliders, dropdowns) for dashboards
GeoDataFrame visualization with interactive layer control
summary: ipyleaflet provides Leaflet.js maps as Jupyter widgets. Map(center, zoom).add_layer(Marker, GeoData, WMSLayer). Two-way Python-JS sync. DrawControl for drawing. Choropleth, Heatmap layers. MIT by Jupyter Widgets. Jupyter-only. Best for interactive geospatial Jupyter dashboards with widget integration. Two-way sync can be fragile. Large GeoDataFrames need simplification. Use folium for static HTML export.
